name: Jean
short_description: BLM rockhounding site south of Las Vegas offering desert quartz, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. Free public land collecting with no permit required.
description:
Jean is a productive rockhounding location on public BLM land in southern Clark County, Nevada, located about 40 miles south of Las Vegas near the California border. This site sits within the Mojave Desert transition zone where Basin and Range geology has created numerous wash systems and exposed rocky outcrops containing quality quartz, chalcedony, jasper, and agate specimens. The terrain is typical of southern Nevada's high desert - sparse vegetation, sandy and rocky footing, and excellent exposure of mineral-bearing formations in wash walls and surface scatter.

Rockhounds come to Jean primarily to collect milky quartz specimens, banded chalcedony, and colorful jasper fragments from desert washes and hillside outcrops. The site benefits from minimal collecting pressure compared to popular areas closer to Las Vegas, making it a good choice for those willing to drive further south. Morning and late afternoon are ideal for rockhounding here, as midday heat can be intense even in cooler months.

This location is well-suited for rockhounders of varying experience levels. The open BLM terrain allows straightforward exploration, and specimens can be found both in active washes and on hillside surfaces without extensive scrambling. However, the remote location and desert heat demand proper preparation, including adequate water, sun protection, and communication devices.
history: The Jean area sits within the southern Basin and Range province where late Miocene to Pliocene volcanic and sedimentary activity deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into quartz, chalcedony, and jasper formations. These minerals occur primarily in wash gravels and as vein material weathered from surrounding bedrock, making them accessible to surface collectors throughout the region.
tips: Search active desert washes first, particularly where water has exposed undercut banks and concentrated heavier mineral material - chalcedony and quartz specimens often concentrate in wash bottoms. Bring a classifier or small screen to sift finer material efficiently. Look for translucent to opaque white, pink, or banded quartz; tan to brown chalcedony showing waxy luster; and rust-red to yellow jasper fragments. Early morning rockhounding maximizes productivity before midday heat becomes oppressive in this exposed desert location.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 2,400 feet in open Mojave Desert terrain. Sparse creosote bush and desert scrub vegetation with rocky footing and sandy washes. Gently rolling topography with intermittent drainage systems. Walking distance from parking areas typically 0.5 to 2 miles. Terrain is loose and rocky with uneven wash bottoms requiring sturdy boots.
best_season: October - April. Desert heat from May through September often exceeds 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making extended collecting sessions dangerous. Winter months offer pleasant daytime temperatures and minimal precipitation risk.
written_directions: From Las Vegas, take US 95 south for approximately 35 miles toward Primm. Watch for small pullouts and rough access roads on the east side of US 95 in the Jean area, roughly 2-3 miles north of the California border. Park at informal pullouts near prominent washes and rockhounding areas visible from the highway. The site is accessible by passenger vehicle but rough secondary roads benefit from higher clearance.
road_conditions: US 95 is paved and well-maintained. Secondary roads and access routes are rough, unpaved, and may have washboard conditions. High-clearance vehicles recommended but not required for main areas.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas along US 95 and near wash access points. No developed parking facilities. Space available for 2-5 vehicles at typical pullouts. Parking is free.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of specimens from archaeological or environmentally sensitive sites. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No removing artifacts or protected resources
Pack out all trash
Respect all public land boundaries and closures
nearest_services: Primm, Nevada (15 miles south) has gas, limited food, and lodging. Las Vegas (40 miles north) has full services. No water available at the site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling desert terrain. Parking is at ground level. Exploration of washes and hillsides requires walking on uneven, rocky, and sandy footing with loose rocks underfoot. No wheelchair access. Sturdy boots strongly recommended. Sun and heat exposure is significant with minimal shade available. Not suitable for those with limited mobility or heat sensitivity.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding near Jean, Nevada?
faq.answer: The Jean area is known for quartz, chalcedony, jasper, and agate. Most specimens are found as desert wash gravels and weathered fragments. Quartz ranges from milky white to translucent varieties, chalcedony appears in banded tan and brown, and jasper occurs as rust-red to yellow fragments. All can be collected as personal use specimens on BLM land with the 25 lb per day limit.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Jean, Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land at Jean. This is personal use collecting only. The Nevada BLM allows collection of common minerals like quartz and chalcedony without advance permission or permits under the general recreation use guidelines.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Jean, Nevada?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Daytime temperatures in these months range from 60-75 degrees Fahrenheit, making it comfortable to spend hours rockhounding in the exposed desert. Summer months (May-September) regularly exceed 100 degrees and are dangerous for outdoor collecting. Avoid summer unless you can hunt very early morning before heat peaks.

faq.question: How far is Jean, Nevada from Las Vegas and is it beginner friendly?
faq.answer: Jean is approximately 40 miles south of Las Vegas via US 95, making it about a 45-minute to 1-hour drive. The site is excellent for beginners because the BLM terrain requires no permits, the open washes are straightforward to search, and quartz and chalcedony specimens are relatively common and easy to spot. The main challenge is the remote location and desert heat rather than rockhounding difficulty.

faq.question: What tools should I bring to rockhound for quartz and chalcedony at Jean?
faq.answer: Bring a good pair of sturdy hiking boots for rocky, uneven terrain. A classifier or small mesh screen helps sift wash gravels efficiently. A geologist's hammer or chisel is useful for specimen extraction. Most importantly, bring at least 2-3 gallons of water per person, sunscreen, a wide-brimmed hat, and a backpack to carry specimens. A GPS device or smartphone with offline maps is recommended due to the remote location.

faq.question: Are dogs allowed rockhounding at Jean, Nevada BLM land?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are allowed on BLM land at Jean. Keep dogs on leash or under control to avoid disturbing wildlife and to prevent them from wandering into desert hazards like mine shafts or snake dens. The remote location and sparse water sources mean bring extra water for your dog, and monitor for heat stress during warm seasons. Paw protection is recommended due to sharp volcanic rock.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding south of Las Vegas. Find quartz, chalcedony, and jasper specimens. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Best October-April.
